skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit AI videos through the inference.sh belt CLI using models for text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar animation, lipsync, upscaling, foley sound, and video merging.
skill.summary: Generate AI videos and media edits with inference.sh video models through the belt CLI.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: The static backtick and weak-crypto findings are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline model identifiers, and the word Description. The real risk is that the skill authorizes Bash(belt *) and instructs agents to submit prompts and user media URLs to external inference.sh model services, which is legitimate for the skill but requires user awareness.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Through belt
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly grants Bash access for belt commands and provides many belt app run examples. This is expected for an inference.sh CLI skill, but it allows external command execution and should be limited to user-approved video generation tasks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated belt commands are direct evidence of CLI execution. The commands are scoped to belt and appear aligned with the skill purpose, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: User Media URLs Sent To External Services
medium_findings.description: The examples submit image_url, audio_url, video_url, and prompt values to hosted model apps. This is necessary for video generation, but it can expose private media URLs, voices, likenesses, and prompts to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Markdown examples clearly show external URL fields passed to belt app run inputs. The use is legitimate but creates a real privacy and data-sharing risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across model tables and Markdown code fences. The reviewed file is documentation, and the backticks mark command examples or model identifiers rather than Ruby execution syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown tables and fenced bash examples, not Ruby source. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak cryptographic algorithm detections correspond to descriptive frontmatter text and a table header. No cryptographic function, cipher selection, hashing code, or credential handling is present at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a natural-language description and line 72 is a Markdown table header containing Description. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation And Service References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to inference.sh documentation, a preview image, and placeholder media URLs. They do not show secret exfiltration endpoints, but they document network dependencies that users should understand.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible documentation links or example placeholders. The pattern is not malicious, but it confirms external network use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Allowlist For External CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed-tools metadata permits Bash commands matching belt, which can authenticate and run remote model apps. This pattern is powerful and should be used only after confirming the user wants to call inference.sh.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool allowlist and quick-start commands are explicit. The scope is restricted to belt, which lowers but does not remove execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External URL Inputs In Generation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples pass media URLs into remote model calls. Private or sensitive media should not be used unless the user accepts the provider data exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly include URL input fields for images, audio, and videos. This is expected functionality, but it is a real data-sharing pattern.

content.value_statement: Video teams need a fast way to choose and run many AI video models. This skill provides ready-to-use belt CLI commands for generation, editing, lipsync, upscaling, and sound workflows.
actual_capabilities: Lists inference.sh video model IDs for text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar, editing, and utility tasks.
Provides belt CLI examples for Veo, Grok, Seedance, HappyHorse, Wan, Fabric, PixVerse, and Topaz workflows.
Shows input fields for prompts, durations, resolutions, generated audio, reference images, audio URLs, and video URLs.
Includes commands for browsing available video apps through the belt CLI.
References related skills for broader inference.sh, image generation, text-to-speech, and social posting workflows.
limitations: Requires an installed inference.sh belt CLI and a logged-in account.
Generated quality, cost, duration, and available parameters depend on each hosted model.
The skill does not validate media rights, consent, likeness permissions, or provider policy compliance.
Private media URLs and prompts may be sent to external services when commands run.

use_cases.title: Produce Short Campaign Videos
use_cases.description: Create social and marketing clips from concise prompts, then compare model outputs for speed and quality.

use_cases.title: Animate Product Or Brand Assets
use_cases.description: Turn approved still images into motion clips and add sound, transitions, or upscaling for publishing.

use_cases.title: Prototype Avatar And Lipsync Flows
use_cases.description: Test talking head, narration, and lipsync models with controlled images and audio files.

prompt_templates.title: Generate A Basic Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a short text-to-video clip about [scene]. Choose a fast model and explain the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Animate An Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to animate this image URL: [image_url]. Make the motion [motion style] and keep the output suitable for [platform].

prompt_templates.title: Create A Talking Avatar
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a talking head video from [portrait_url] and [audio_url]. Pick a lipsync or avatar model and note privacy considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Multi-Step Video Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a pipeline for [campaign goal]. Include generation, editing, foley, upscaling, and final merge steps with model choices.

output_examples.input: Create a five-second forest flyover video with a cinematic style.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a fast Veo model, prepares a concise prompt, and runs a belt command for text-to-video generation.

output_examples.input: Animate a product image and add realistic showroom motion.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses an image-to-video model, includes the provided image URL, and describes expected motion and output constraints.

output_examples.input: Make a talking avatar from a portrait and voiceover.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends an avatar or lipsync model and warns that the portrait and audio are sent to the external provider.

best_practices: Confirm the user approves external inference.sh calls before running belt commands.
Use public or approved media URLs, especially for faces, voices, brands, and client assets.
Start with fast or economical models, then rerun promising prompts on higher-quality models.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private media, faces, voices, or unreleased assets without permission.
Do not run generation commands before explaining expected cost, model choice, and data sharing.
Do not assume every model supports the same duration, audio, resolution, or input fields.

faq.question: Does this skill generate videos directly inside Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. It prepares and runs inference.sh belt CLI commands that call hosted video generation apps.

faq.question: What must be installed before using it?
faq.answer: The inference.sh belt CLI must be installed, and the user must authenticate with belt login.

faq.question: Can it use images, audio, and existing videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include image-to-video, lipsync, avatar, editing, foley, upscaling, and merge workflows.

faq.question: Are media URLs sent to an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. Commands send prompts and media URLs to inference.sh model apps, so sensitive inputs need user approval.

faq.question: Does it choose among multiple video models?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents model IDs and common use cases for Veo, Seedance, HappyHorse, Wan, Grok, and other models.

faq.question: Can it guarantee video quality or exact timing?
faq.answer: No. Output quality, duration, audio support, and parameter behavior depend on the selected hosted model.
